patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: test qgroup deletion races with squota writes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds four test cases to the Btrfs file system to cover various race conditions between qgroup deletion and squota writes. The tests aim to identify potential bugs in the handling of extent ownership references (EXTENT_OWNER_REF) when a subvolume is deleted or its qgroup is destroyed. The scenarios tested include freeing an extent whose owner qgroup is gone, creating an extent while the owning subvolume is being deleted, destroying a live subvolume's qgroup with delayed refs, and destroying a qgroup belonging to a still-mounted subvolume. The patch ensures that these operations are properly handled by the Btrfs kernel to prevent underflows or transaction aborts.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: inline enable_quota helper in test 301

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: inline enable_quota helper in test 301

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch inlines the enable_quota helper function in test 301, removing a dead-weight dispatch and indirection to directly call 'btrfs quota enable --simple'. The change is limited to this specific test and does not affect other parts of the btrfs codebase.

review_comments.author: Zorro Lang
review_comments.summary: Expressed neutrality towards the patch, suggesting separate review of this patch and its companion. Did not raise any specific technical concerns or objections.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 05:43:33PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> The enable_quota helper in btrfs/301 takes a mode argument ('n', 's',
> or anything else) to switch between "no quota", "simple quota", and
> "full quota" enable.  Only the simple-quota path is ever exercised by
> this test, so the dispatch is dead weight and the indirection just
> obscures the actual btrfs command being run.
> 
> Inline the two call sites to call 'btrfs quota enable --simple'
> directly and drop the helper.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---

Hahah, to be honest, the title '[PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests' gave me
a minor heart attack. I thought it was a critical bug report about fstests
accidentally deleting itself while running :-D

>  tests/btrfs/301 | 14 ++------------
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/tests/btrfs/301 b/tests/btrfs/301
> index 1f72a97b..31243aab 100755
> --- a/tests/btrfs/301
> +++ b/tests/btrfs/301
> @@ -169,16 +169,6 @@ do_enospc_falloc()
>  	do_falloc $file $sz
>  }
>  
> -enable_quota()
> -{
> -	local mode=$1
> -
> -	[ $mode == "n" ] && return
> -	arg=$([ $mode == "s" ] && echo "--simple")
> -
> -	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable $arg $SCRATCH_MNT
> -}
> -
>  get_subvid()
>  {
>  	_btrfs_get_subvolid $SCRATCH_MNT subv
> @@ -198,7 +188,7 @@ prepare()
>  {
>  	_scratch_mkfs >> $seqres.full
>  	_scratch_mount
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT
>  	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG subvolume create $subv >> $seqres.full
>  	local subvid=$(get_subvid)
>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
> @@ -421,7 +411,7 @@ enable_mature()
>  	# Sync before enabling squotas to reliably *not* count the writes
>  	# we did before enabling.
>  	sync
> -	enable_quota "s"
> +	$BTRFS_UTIL_PROG quota enable --simple $SCRATCH_MNT

This change doesn't seem related to patch 2/2. I think we can review
these 2 patches separately.

For this patch, I'm fine with or without it. If the btrfs list prefers
to have it, or move it to be a common helper, I'm good with that.

Thanks,
Zorro

>  	set_subvol_limit $subvid $limit
>  	_scratch_cycle_mount
>  	usage=$(get_subvol_usage $subvid)
> -- 
> 2.54.0
> 
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] squota delete fstests
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series resurrects a previous attempt to land tests for squota qgroup deletion edge cases, incorporating additional testing and feedback.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/4] btrfs: swallow btrfs_record_squota_delta() enoent
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to harden the squota deletion mechanism in Btrfs, making it impossible for an extent to be accounted to a qgroup that outlives its qgroup. The author has re-considered their previous stance on this issue due to recent bugs and believes adding extra protection is necessary. The patch modifies the btrfs_record_squota_delta() function to swallow ENOENT errors, effectively masking real bugs.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Requested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE to catch real bugs in debug builds, rather than masking them by swallowing ENOENT errors.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/5/13 05:04, Boris Burkov 写道:
> I thought that it was likely I could harden squota deletion to the point
> that it was impossible to end up with an extent accounted to a qgroup
> outliving its qgroup. Several recent bugs have made me re-consider that
> position.
> 
> Ultimately, this is a tradeoff between short term stability and long
> term strictness, but I think given that there could be another layer of
> bugs behind the 2-3 I just fixed, I would feel much more confident in
> people using squotas if the risk was "your values can get a bit out of
> whack which you can fix by deleting stuff or
> disabling/re-enabling/repairing" vs "it will abort your filesystem".
> 
> As the final nail in the coffin, the Meta production kernel was lacking
> earlier fixes from me and Qu regarding subvol qgroup lifetime, so this
> is what we have been testing at scale, so I think at least for now
> upstream should have the same extra layer of protection.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>   fs/btrfs/qgroup.c | 2 +-
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> index a93d49015fc7..f9e5005028dc 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> @@ -4959,7 +4959,7 @@ int btrfs_record_squota_delta(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
>   	spin_lock(&fs_info->qgroup_lock);
>   	qgroup = find_qgroup_rb(fs_info, root);
>   	if (!qgroup) {
> -		ret = -ENOENT;
> +		ret = 0;

I appreciate that end users won't be bothered with transaction abort, 
but on the other hand, this seems to mask real bugs.

Can we add a WARN_ON_ONCE(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BTRFS_DEBUG))) so that we 
can catch such problems for debug builds?

Thanks,
Qu>   		goto out;
>   	}
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/4] btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses quota accounting issues in Btrfs, specifically a problem where metadata tree block allocations can be undercounted in certain scenarios, leading to qgroups becoming permanently orphaned after subvolume deletion. The patch introduces clamping of rfer and excl values to zero on underflow to mitigate this issue.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to increase the likelihood of detecting undercounted metadata tree block allocations in fstests.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/5/13 05:04, Boris Burkov 写道:
> Simple quota accounting can undercount metadata tree block allocations
> in certain scenarios. When an undercounted subvolume is deleted and its
> tree blocks freed, the free deltas decrement rfer/excl past zero,
> wrapping the u64 to a value near U64_MAX.
> 
> Once wrapped, can_delete_squota_qgroup() sees non-zero rfer and refuses
> to delete the qgroup. The qgroup becomes permanently orphaned in the
> quota tree, since there is no subvolume left to generate frees that
> would bring the counter back to zero.
> 
> While we ultimately want to fix any mis-accounting at the source, it is
> also helpful and worthwhile to mitigate the damage by clamping rfer and
> excl to zero on underflow rather than allowing the u64 to wrap. This at
> least allows us to clean up the messed up qgroups on subvol deletion.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> ---
>   fs/btrfs/qgroup.c | 15 +++++++++++++--
>   1 file changed, 13 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> index 96746ca19e17..a93d49015fc7 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/qgroup.c
> @@ -4968,8 +4968,19 @@ int btrfs_record_squota_delta(struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info,
>   	list_for_each_entry(qg, &qgroup_list, iterator) {
>   		struct btrfs_qgroup_list *glist;
>   
> -		qg->excl += num_bytes * sign;
> -		qg->rfer += num_bytes * sign;
> +		ASSERT(qg->excl == qg->rfer);
> +		if (sign < 0 && qg->excl < num_bytes) {
> +			btrfs_warn(fs_info,
> +				   "squota underflow qg %hu/%llu excl %llu num_bytes %llu",
> +				   btrfs_qgroup_level(qg->qgroupid),
> +				   btrfs_qgroup_subvolid(qg->qgroupid),
> +				   qg->excl, num_bytes);

This may not be noisy enough for fstests to catch.

Maybe a WARN_ON_ONCE()?

Thanks,
Qu

> +			qg->excl = 0;
> +			qg->rfer = 0;
> +		} else {
> +			qg->excl += num_bytes * sign;
> +			qg->rfer += num_bytes * sign;
> +		}
>   		qgroup_dirty(fs_info, qg);
>   
>   		list_for_each_entry(glist, &qg->groups, next_group)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/4] btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/4] btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem in Btrfs where quota accounting is incorrect when enabling quotas for the first time. When the first transaction that enables quotas completes, it sets a flag to indicate that quotas are enabled, but this can cause a deadlock and lead to incorrect quota values. The patch introduces two changes: first, it sets the enable generation to the next transaction's ID instead of the current one, ensuring that extents written during the enabling transaction are not accounted for; second, it adds a new flag to record squota deltas for extents written between the end of the enabling transaction and when the quotas-enabled flag is set. This fixes an underflow issue where metadata tree blocks would be freed prematurely, causing mismatched usage errors in btrfs check.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/4] btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/4] btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Btrfs file system where deleting a subvolume quota (squota) group could break an invariant, leading to inconsistent state. The fix unifies the deletion check logic for both full qgroups and squotas, ensuring that the correct checks are performed before deleting a subvolume quota group. This prevents potential issues with shared extents outliving their owning subvolumes. The patch modifies the `can_delete_parent_qgroup` function to perform additional checks specific to squota groups, including verifying that there is no root and checking the usage of the parent qgroup.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] btrfs: squota lifetime fixes
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to strengthen the enforcement of a crucial invariant in btrfs squotas: 'a squota qgroup must outlast extents that are accounted to it'. It consists of two fixes for squota lifetime bugs and two patches that reduce the consequences of errors in lifetime management. The series also includes additional improvements to btrfs check and fstests.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues in the last two patches and suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to catch real bugs for debug builds. Considered the patch series overall and found it looks good, but minor comments were made.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Expressed general agreement with the patch series and suggested adding it to for-next.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 12:34:52PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> A recent report of read-only filesystems since enabling squotas:
> 
> https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/adnBhWfJQ1n3hZC8@merlins.org/
> 
> led me to investigate a handful of issues in squotas and find some trivial and
> not so trivial bugs in their lifetime management. The aim of the series is to
> strengthen enforcement of the invariant: "a squota qgroup must outlast extents
> that are accounted to it".
> 
> This series consists of two fixes to squota lifetime bugs and two patches that
> reduce the consequences of errors in lifetime management from full fs goes
> read-only abort to "warning and inaccurate squotas which can be deleted
> eventually"
> 
> Additional improvements to btrfs check and fstests to follow.
> 
> Boris Burkov (4):
>   btrfs: check for subvolume before deleting squota qgroup
>   btrfs: fix squota accounting during enable generation
>   btrfs: clamp to avoid squota underflow
>   btrfs: swallow btrfs_record_squota_delta() enoent

Looks good to me, as much I can understand it. Please add it to
for-next.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention and release events. The patch uses an arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, only a single NOP instruction is added to the hot path, minimizing performance impact. When enabled, the contention check, trace call, and unlock are combined in an out-of-line function to further minimize hot path impact. The patch also includes estimates of binary size increases due to this change.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint is not properly disabled when the lock is already unlocked, and suggested adding a check to ensure the lock is locked before tracing.

The reviewer suggested removing a phrase from the patch description that is considered unnecessary in Git change logs.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and may cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of returning immediately, the function should be called directly

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out a redundant call to queued_spin_release() in the code and requested its removal.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:34 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Remove the ", introduced in the previous commit," That's useless in git
change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you
need to duplicate the code. Even though it's a one liner, it can cause bugs
in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra pointed out that the patch adds unnecessary instructions to the hot path, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the initial NOP, increasing binary size by 12-15 bytes. He also disagreed with the claim that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n configurations opt against binary size optimization on x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It refactors the existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to use this new function. This change has no functional impact and is a preparatory step for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock().

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Requested a change in the changelog to use more descriptive language instead of 'next commit'.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:33 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Introduce queued_spin_release() as an arch-overridable unlock primitive,
> and make queued_spin_unlock() a generic wrapper around it.
> 
> This is a preparatory refactoring for the next commit, which adds
> contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock().

In change logs, do not use "next commit". Instead say something like:

  In preparation for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to
  queued_spin_unlock(), refactor the code to allow out of line calls when
  the tracepoint is enabled.

Or something like that.

-- Steve



> 
> Rename the existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on
> x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to queued_spin_release().
> 
> No functional change.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Reviewed-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks (mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks) to include calls to trace_contended_release(). This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the __queued_read_unlock() and __queued_write_unlock() functions to be extracted from the queued read/write lock implementation in qrwlock.h. The goal is to prepare for future instrumentation of contended release events.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Mentioned that using 'next commit' in change logs is not ideal, but did not provide further feedback on the patch itself.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> This is a preparatory refactoring for the next commit, which adds

Same thing about using "next commit" in change logs.

-- Steve

> contended_release tracepoint instrumentation and needs to call the
> unlock from both traced and non-traced paths.
> 
> No functional change.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>
> ---
>  include/asm-generic/qrwlock.h | 20 +++++++++++++++-----

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extends the contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, using an out-of-line traced unlock approach similar to that used for queued spinlocks.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about duplicated code in the patch series. Suggested removing the actual locking logic from the traced unlock variants and only tracing the release event.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified, and the slowpath is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long a lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not be accurate for reader/writer locks, as it fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, which could lead to spurious events.

The reviewer suggested rephrasing the patch description to use more descriptive language, specifically recommending against using 'next commit' and instead suggesting a more detailed explanation of the code refactoring.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:33 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

In change logs, do not use "next commit". Instead say something like:

  In preparation for adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation to
  queued_spin_unlock(), refactor the code to allow out of line calls when
  the tracepoint is enabled.

Or something like that.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not work correctly for reader/writer locks due to its current implementation, which fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, and requested clarification on this behavior

The reviewer requested removal of a phrase from the patch description, specifically the phrase 'introduced in the previous commit', which is deemed unnecessary in a Git change log.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt suggested removing a 'return;' statement from the code, arguing that it is unnecessary and can cause bugs in the future if modified; instead of using the return statement, he recommended calling the function do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock) directly

Reviewer Steven Rostedt requested removal of a duplicate call to queued_spin_release()
sentiment_signals: potential issue with reader/writer lock behavior
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:34 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Remove the ", introduced in the previous commit," That's useless in git
change logs.

---

Get rid of the "return;". What does it save you? It just makes it that you
need to duplicate the code. Even though it's a one liner, it can cause bugs
in the future if this changes. You could call the function:

  do_trace_queued_spin_release_traced(lock);

---

And then remove the duplicate call of "queued_spin_release()" here.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation does not handle the case where a lock is released while it's being held by a task that has already been scheduled out, and requested the addition of a check to ensure that the lock is still held when the tracepoint fires.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt pointed out that the patch description should not use 'next commit' in the changelog, echoing a previous comment from another reviewer.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:35 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Same thing about using "next commit" in change logs.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation may not be thread-safe due to the use of a static branch check, and requested the patch author to consider using a different approach.

Reviewer Steven Rostedt simply echoed the original message 'ditto' without providing any additional context or feedback, indicating no clear signal.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue,  5 May 2026 17:09:36 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Ditto.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra expressed uncertainty about the value of adding contended_release tracepoint instrumentation for qspinlock and rwlock, questioning whether it's worth the overhead.
review_comments.raw_body:
And for qspinlock.

I am really not sure this is worth the overhead for qspinlock / rwlock.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Peter Zijlstra noted that the patch adds unnecessary instructions (12-15 bytes) to the contended_release tracepoint instrumentation, specifically a CALL and JMP instruction in addition to the original MOV instruction, which he considers 'quite ludicrous'. He also disagreed with the assumption that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size on x86, stating that the unlock is smaller than a function call.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is not quite accurate. You add the (5byte) NOP for the static
branch, but then you also add another 5 bytes for the CALL and at least
another 2 bytes (possibly 5) for a JMP back into the previous stream.
That is 12-15 bytes added to what was a single MOV instruction.

That is quite ludicrous.

I disagree that UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK=n opts against binary size. For x86
the unlock is smaller than a function call.


I really don't see how this is worth it.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/2] virtio-balloon: extended stats and push mode

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/2] virtio-balloon: extended stats and push mode

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/2] virtio-balloon: extend stats with memory composition and pressure data
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the virtio-balloon statistics to include memory composition and pressure data, allowing for more informed proactive ballooning decisions. It adds 11 new statistics tags to the existing balloon stats virtqueue, including metrics for dirty pages, writeback pages, anonymous pages, inactive file LRU, slab reclaimable memory, working set refaults, and PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics for memory and IO pressure. The patch also exports psi_system for module builds when CONFIG_VIRTIO_BALLOON=m with CONFIG_PSI=y.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/2] virtio-balloon: add stats push mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the virtio-balloon driver called stats push mode. When enabled, the guest pushes statistics to the host at regular intervals instead of the default pull model. This provides two benefits: it allows the host to detect if the guest is unresponsive and it ensures that latency-sensitive consumers receive fresh statistics at a guaranteed cadence without needing to poll. The patch introduces a new configuration field stats_push_interval_ms, which specifies the interval in milliseconds between pushes. If this field is set to 0, the default pull model is used. The patch also includes changes to the driver to support the new feature and ensure that it works correctly with existing code.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series extends the virtio-balloon stats virtqueue to include new tags for memory composition and pressure data, providing more accurate reclaimable estimates and balloon sizing information. It also adds an optional push mode for guest-initiated stat reporting, allowing hosts to configure guests to push stats at a guaranteed cadence without waiting for host confirmation.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 00/22] mm: Add __GFP_UNMAPPED
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch series aims to add a new GFP flag __GFP_UNMAPPED for unmapped memory allocations, but the discussion revolves around the TLB issue and its implications on kernel direct mapping.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the TLB issue and its implications on kernel direct mapping. Suggested that the problem might not be related to the allocator but rather to incorrect TLB flushing.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 13, 2026 at 07:38:01PM +0200, Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) wrote:
> On 5/13/26 19:28, Gregory Price wrote:
> > 
> > Hm.  I'm not quite wrapping my head around the TLB issue fully.
> > 
> > If there's no kernel direct mapping, and there's no userland mapping,
> > the stale TLB entry comes from... the page formerly being present in the
> > page tables and a stale TLB entry lying about after the page is freed?
> 
> It's the direct mapping, we assume it's always there and unchanged, and only
> kernel can access the contents through it. So nobody flushes it when freeing
> any pages. Userspace processes can't exploit anything stale there, in
> absence of kernel's UAF bugs (or e.g. Meltdown like cpu bugs).
> 

Ah, I follow.

If everything is default-unmapped, then you don't have to worry about
this issue - except when a stolen block is returned or an ephemeral
mapping is unmapped after the operation.

pivoting...

On the GFP front, i wonder if you could factor out the core of
alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() and add alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof()
which adds (alloc_flags |= ALLOC_UNMAPPED) instead of adding
__GFP_UNMAPPED.

I have been considering something similar for __GFP_PRIVATE, but this
has the added downside of increasing the surface of the buddy for each
new narrow use case (in my case, private nodes, in this case unmapped
allocations).

unless of course we nip that in the bud with something like

struct page *
alloc_pages_special(enum buddy_context ctxt, gfp_t gfp_mask, ...)
{
    switch (ctxt) {
       ... internal-only details about how that case is handled ...
    }
}

and just go ahead and allow the buddy to grow internally without adding
new gfp flags or an infinite number of interfaces.

Of course that means users have to know the context in which they're
being allocated.  Right now you can kind of "transiently cheat" by
passing a GFP flag through a bunch of interfaces and that makes certain
allocations reachable - but maybe we should not be encouraging that kind
of design for these kinds of allocator extensions?

Just spitballing here on the GFP issue since that seems to be coming to
a head on multiple fronts.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Clarified the role of kernel direct mapping and its relationship to TLB flushing. Stated that userspace processes cannot exploit stale TLB entries in the absence of kernel UAF bugs.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Proposed a new approach to handle GFP flags and suggested factoring out the core of alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() to add alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof(). Discussed the trade-offs of increasing the surface area of the buddy for each new use case.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 13, 2026 at 07:38:01PM +0200, Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) wrote:
> On 5/13/26 19:28, Gregory Price wrote:
> > 
> > Hm.  I'm not quite wrapping my head around the TLB issue fully.
> > 
> > If there's no kernel direct mapping, and there's no userland mapping,
> > the stale TLB entry comes from... the page formerly being present in the
> > page tables and a stale TLB entry lying about after the page is freed?
> 
> It's the direct mapping, we assume it's always there and unchanged, and only
> kernel can access the contents through it. So nobody flushes it when freeing
> any pages. Userspace processes can't exploit anything stale there, in
> absence of kernel's UAF bugs (or e.g. Meltdown like cpu bugs).
> 

Ah, I follow.

If everything is default-unmapped, then you don't have to worry about
this issue - except when a stolen block is returned or an ephemeral
mapping is unmapped after the operation.

pivoting...

On the GFP front, i wonder if you could factor out the core of
alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() and add alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof()
which adds (alloc_flags |= ALLOC_UNMAPPED) instead of adding
__GFP_UNMAPPED.

I have been considering something similar for __GFP_PRIVATE, but this
has the added downside of increasing the surface of the buddy for each
new narrow use case (in my case, private nodes, in this case unmapped
allocations).

unless of course we nip that in the bud with something like

struct page *
alloc_pages_special(enum buddy_context ctxt, gfp_t gfp_mask, ...)
{
    switch (ctxt) {
       ... internal-only details about how that case is handled ...
    }
}

and just go ahead and allow the buddy to grow internally without adding
new gfp flags or an infinite number of interfaces.

Of course that means users have to know the context in which they're
being allocated.  Right now you can kind of "transiently cheat" by
passing a GFP flag through a bunch of interfaces and that makes certain
allocations reachable - but maybe we should not be encouraging that kind
of design for these kinds of allocator extensions?

Just spitballing here on the GFP issue since that seems to be coming to
a head on multiple fronts.

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 00/31] mm/virtio: skip redundant zeroing of host-zeroed pages
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch series to skip redundant zeroing of host-zeroed pages in virtio mm code

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Patch author acknowledges being guilty of similar behavior in the past and apologizes for not having more time to review patches thoroughly.

review_comments.author: Michael S. Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer acknowledges Gregory's apology and thanks him for his efforts, but also jokingly suggests that Gregory should wait at least a week before sending more patches.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 00/22] mm: Add __GFP_UNMAPPED
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new flag __GFP_UNMAPPED to the Linux kernel's memory allocation system, allowing for more efficient handling of unmapped memory allocations. The current approach involves adding an additional level of indirection, increasing the migratetype matrix. An alternative solution proposed by Gregory Price is to have the buddy unmap memory after zeroing and allocation, and remap it on free. This would simplify the process and hide the changes from existing users.

review_comments.author: Brendan Jackman
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the current design only supports one migratetype being unmapped, and requested a more general solution that can handle arbitrary migratetypes.

Reviewer noted that the proposed __GFP_UNMAPPED mechanism may not be universally applicable due to the presence of guest memory, where a user address is not available for write() operations.

Reviewer noted that the proposed __GFP_UNMAPPED mechanism is not primarily about default memory state, but rather about batching and reducing TLB flushes during allocation; they suggested unmap-on-alloc approach as a cleaner alternative to achieve this goal.

Reviewer noted that doing unmapping on a per-allocation basis would result in most map-unmap cycles being pointless, as most pages are expected to be unmapped, and suggested considering the opposite mechanism of unmap-on-free instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Because the fact that only one migratetype currently supports being
unmapped is a temporary happenstance of the guest_memfd usecase. In
general, this needs to support having unmapped variants of ~arbitrary
migratetypes.

---

Yeah, I actually only noticed that yesterday due to your posts on that
thread! I need to investigate it further. My assumption has always been
that this isn't a general solution because we don't always _have_ a user
address (e.g. for guest_memfd it's important that we can populate the
memory via write(), so there's no user address), but it's pretty likely
I'm missing something there.

---

That would be cleaner indeed, but the key question here isn't about the
default state of memory here, it's about batching.

The reason we need to do it at the block granularity is that a TLB flush
every time we allocate one of these pages is a performance nonstarter -
that's actually the entire point of this series. If you can afford a TLB
flush per allocation then you don't need __GFP_UNMAPPED for the
guest_memfd usecase, the existing direct map removal series [0] is
already fine.

[0] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410151746.61150-1-kalyazin@amazon.com/

---

Hopefully my prev paragraph explained that it's not viable anyway, but:
if we _did_ do the [un]mapping on a per-allocation basis, the
disadvantage of unmap-on-alloc is just that we expect most pages in the
system to be unmapped. So the majority of map-unmap cycles are pointless
(map on free, but we're probably gonna unmap again on allloc).

Cheers,
Brendan

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the stale TLB entry issue might not be related to the __GFP_UNMAPPED patch, but rather a problem of incorrect TLB flushing in page freeing or unmapping. Author plans to spend more time understanding the issue.

Author acknowledges reviewer's preference for block-granularity map/unmap operations and asks for cost data on page vs block operations, indicating a need for further revision to optimize the approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm.  I'm not quite wrapping my head around the TLB issue fully.

If there's no kernel direct mapping, and there's no userland mapping,
the stale TLB entry comes from... the page formerly being present in the
page tables and a stale TLB entry lying about after the page is freed?

If that's the case, that sounds more like someone isn't flushing the TLB
entry correctly when the page is freed or unmapped (for a transient
mermap situation), rather than an issue to be handled by the allocator.

I think I just need to spend a little more time understanding the TLB
issue.

---

That tracks.  So whatever the case, it seems like you prefer
block-granularity map/unmap operations, rather than per-allocation.

Do you have numbers on what the cost of map/unmap on a page vs block is?

Whatever the case is - you're pushing that cost onto *someone*, so
having that data would at least let us know the value of pushing most
memory to be unmapped by default rather than the opposite.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the direct mapping is assumed to be always present and unchanged, and only the kernel can access its contents; they questioned whether this assumption holds true in all scenarios, specifically mentioning UAF bugs or CPU bugs like Meltdown.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's the direct mapping, we assume it's always there and unchanged, and only
kernel can access the contents through it. So nobody flushes it when freeing
any pages. Userspace processes can't exploit anything stale there, in
absence of kernel's UAF bugs (or e.g. Meltdown like cpu bugs).

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed concerns about adding a new level of indirection (GFP flags) instead of using existing mechanisms, proposed an alternative approach by factoring out alloc_frozen_pages_noprof() and introducing alloc_unmapped_pages_noprof(), but also suggested considering a more radical change to the buddy interface with context-dependent allocation functions. No clear resolution or fix planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah, I follow.

If everything is default-unmapped, then you don't have to worry about
this issue - except when a stolen block is returned or an ephemeral
mapping is unmapped after the operation.

pivoting...

{
    switch (ctxt) {
       ... internal-only details about how that case is handled ...
    }
}

and just go ahead and allow the buddy to grow internally without adding
new gfp flags or an infinite number of interfaces.

Of course that means users have to know the context in which they're
being allocated.  Right now you can kind of "transiently cheat" by
passing a GFP flag through a bunch of interfaces and that makes certain
allocations reachable - but maybe we should not be encouraging that kind
of design for these kinds of allocator extensions?

Just spitballing here on the GFP issue since that seems to be coming to

~Gregory
